narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70 and an LSAT score of 34, you've clearly put in significant effort to build a strong academic foundation, and we appreciate the dedication that brought you here. Your background includes lower-middle family income and plans to attend law school part-time, which reflects a practical approach to balancing commitments. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 51% probability of failure for you—meaning it saw a slight risk that tipped the scales toward rejection of your application. This decision was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which stood out as the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, well below the typical student's 5th or 6th decile position. While your strong undergraduate GPA was a major positive influence, pulling strongly in favor of passing the bar compared to the average of 3.21, it wasn't quite enough to offset that Year 3 performance dip. Your LSAT score also helped by nudging the odds toward success, though it was a bit lower than the dataset average of around 37. Being in the part-time track added a modest push toward the failure prediction, as most students (over 90%) pursue full-time and tend to show stronger bar outcomes in the model's view. Your family income level provided a small favorable nudge, aligning with patterns where lower-middle backgrounds don't hinder success. Overall, your profile compares favorably in undergraduate performance and test scores to typical applicants but is held back by that lower Year 3 decile relative to peers. To strengthen future applications, consider ways to boost your later-year grades, perhaps through targeted study habits or additional coursework that mirrors full-time rigor. You might also retake the LSAT to push closer to or above the average, building on your already impressive GPA. We're rooting for you—many students in similar situations turn things around with focused improvements, and law school doors can reopen with a refined profile. Keep pursuing your goals; your determination as a white male from a modest background positions you well for resilience in this field.
